paper_title: Study of Diatom Community Composition in Lake Sinclair

methods.description: This method describes the process of collecting and preserving algal samples from Lake Sinclair to study the effects of temperature on diatom community composition.

steps.description: Two sites on Lake Sinclair were chosen for comparison of the temperature effects. One in the immediate 'warmer' area of the power plant, and the other was approximately 1.7 km to the south representing ambient temperatures.
steps.searchable_summary: select sampling sites based on temperature proximity to power plant

steps.description: The cold and warm locations were sampled for seven months. There were a total of 15 samples taken from each sample site.
steps.searchable_summary: collect samples from designated sites over seven months

steps.description: Once collected, the samples were transported to the laboratory in a cooler with ice and immediately preserved. Algal samples were preserved with 3% formaldehyde for later processing and identification.
steps.searchable_summary: preserve samples with formaldehyde for lab analysis

methods.description: This method involves measuring various water quality parameters at the sampling sites to assess environmental conditions.

steps.description: Algal samples were collected approximately every 14 days from August 2008 to February 2009, along with triplicate measures of water temperature, pH, and conductivity with YSI 556 MPS instrument.
steps.searchable_summary: measure water temperature, pH, and conductivity biweekly

steps.description: Turbidity was measured using a LaMotte ® Portable Turbidity meter.
steps.searchable_summary: measure water turbidity using portable meter

steps.description: A Secchi disc (Carolina biological Inc.) was used to evaluate the depth of the photic zone.
steps.searchable_summary: use Secchi disc to measure photic zone depth

methods.description: This method describes the collection and analysis of water samples for nutrient content to understand the chemical environment of the lake.

steps.description: Water for nutrients analyses was collected in 125 ml acid washed bottles and sent to the University of Georgia Marine Extension Service and University of Georgia commercial chemical analyses lab in Athens, Georgia.
steps.searchable_summary: collect water samples for nutrient analysis

steps.description: Water samples were analyzed for nutrients including Ammonium, Nitrate/Nitrite, Total Nitrogen, and total Phosphorus.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze water samples for nutrient content

methods.description: This method involves the preparation and analysis of diatom samples to study their community composition.

steps.description: Lab analysis of algal diatom assemblage composition was performed using cleaned (digested) samples preserved on permanent slides.

The samples were cleaned of organic matter in 50% nitric acid for 2 hours. Then less than one gram of potassium dichromate was added to them as a catalyzer, heated for approximately 5 minutes and left to cool for 30 minutes.
steps.searchable_summary: clean diatom samples with nitric acid and potassium dichromate

steps.description: Permanent diatom slides were prepared by acid cleaning, to increase the clarity of observing diatoms, and then mounted in Naphrax resin.
steps.searchable_summary: mount cleaned diatom samples on slides with Naphrax resin

steps.description: At least 300 valves were counted from each sample using the 1000x objective lens on an Aus JenaLumar scope.
Diatoms were identified to the lowest taxonomic unit using standard identification keys and following standard procedures for diatom identifications.
steps.searchable_summary: identify diatoms to lowest taxonomic unit
